attributes.stem:
A 35-year old man presents to the General Practice with a two-day history of pain in the right side of his scrotum. He has no other symptoms of urethral discharge or rash. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. He states he has had only one sexual partner over the past ten years.

On examination, he appears uncomfortable. His pulse rate is 80/min, blood pressure 118/76 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.4°C. The right hemiscrotum is reddened and tender to touch. There is an area of tenderness immediately above the testis, which is in its normal lie, but the testis itself is not tender. The cremasteric reflex is present bilaterally.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Torsion of the hydatid of Morgagni
attributes.explanation:
The symptoms and physical findings are consistent with epididymitis - a reddened hemiscrotum with pain developing over 1-2 days, tenderness specifically above the testis (where the epididymis is located), and fever. The gradual onset over days rather than hours, normal testicular lie, and preserved cremasteric reflex are all features that support this diagnosis. The commonest cause of epididymitis in older men is reflux of infected urine, usually due to urinary retention. The most common organism in men >35 years old is Escherichia coli. In men <35 years, sexually transmitted infections must be considered, with Chlamydia trachomatis being one of the commonest infectious agents or Neisseria gonorrhoea.

Torsion of the testis typically presents with sudden onset of severe scrotal pain, often with nausea and vomiting. On examination, the testis is usually high-riding with an abnormal lie, and the cremasteric reflex is absent. The gradual onset over two days and the presence of the cremasteric reflex make testicular torsion unlikely in this case. Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency requiring immediate intervention and should always be excluded for any presentation of acute scrotal pain.

Torsion of the hydatid of Morgagni (appendix testis) can present with acute scrotal pain but is typically less severe than testicular torsion. It often affects prepubertal boys and may show a characteristic 'blue dot sign' on examination, which was not described in this patient. The presentation with fever and the age of the patient make this diagnosis less likely.

Thrombosed varicocele can present with acute pain; however, on examination, an engorged venous plexus would be visible or palpable, which was not described in this patient. Varicoceles are more common on the left side due to anatomical differences in venous drainage, whereas this patient has right-sided symptoms.

Orchitis refers to inflammation of the testis itself and often coexists with epididymitis (epididymo-orchitis). However, in this case, the testis is specifically noted not to be tender, and the tenderness is localized to the area above the testis where the epididymis is located, making isolated epididymitis more likely than orchitis.
learningPoints: Epididymitis typically presents with gradual onset scrotal pain, tenderness above the testis, and fever.
In men under 35 years, sexually transmitted infections (particularly Chlamydia and gonorrhoea) are common causes of epididymitis.
Testicular torsion must be excluded in any man with acute scrotal pain as it is a surgical emergency.
references: Srinath H. Acute scrotal Pain. Australian Family Physician. 2013; 42(11): 790-92.  Available at:  https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2013/november/acute-scrotal-pain
Ong JJ, Bourne C, Dean JA et al. Australian sexually transmitted infection (STI) management guidelines for use in primary care 2022 update. Sexual Health
